title: Lesson 362
transcript: Here's a really common security challenge related to access management that many organizations have. Let's say you've bought some application from a vendor, and it's a complex application, and you're probably gonna pay for some sort of support contract to allow that vendor to remotely log, well, first of all, to help you install the application, you're probably gonna create an account in the application for the vendor that allows them to remotely log into the application and usually gives them root privileges so they can help you walk you through the installation process. And then these accounts are often maintained for years because you've got an ongoing support contract where let's say either you need to install a new update to the application or you're having some sort of issue with the application, you want that vendor to be able to remotely log in and help you troubleshoot the problem. And so it's really common that organizations will have quite a few of these remotely accessible accounts that provide root access into an application and this is the most dangerous type of good account to have around right an account that can be remotely accessible that provides root privileges to a system By default, these accounts should always be disabled, and they should only be enabled for a short period of time when that vendor's coming in, helping to support the application, and then right away that account should be disabled again. But I can tell you from years of conducting audits and looking at these accounts many, many times, most organizations don't remember to disable them again. And so it was really common, just from my own experience when I was conducting access reviews. You know, you'd ask for a list of all the access to an application and you'd find these vendor accounts, remotely accessible, root access, that were sitting there, enabled. Dangerous. And so this is what just-in-time access is meant to address. Just-in-time access is essentially the idea that it's a tool that will ensure that these accounts are disabled and other sort of privileged accounts are automatically disabled all the time. And these accounts will only be enabled when they're needed. And usually only for a short period of time, maybe a few hours, and then those accounts, because this is an automated process, will be automatically disabled again. And so one of the big security benefits of just-in-time access is that by default, all of these sort of dangerous accounts are always gonna be disabled by default. And they will only be enabled for a short period of time when they're needed. And the way that just-in-time access systems typically works is that this is an automated process. There'll be a process that a vendor or even an employee could go through to say, okay, I need this access to this system to do this. And there can be an automated approval process in place where once they've gone through that automated approval process, This account will be enabled for just a short period of time and then automatically disabled. That's the idea of just-in-time access.
